title: Quantum Circuit Synthesis Using an Exact T Library
authors: Hanyu Wang, Mingfei Yu, Xinrui Wu, Jason Cong
abstract: In fault-tolerant quantum circuit synthesis, T gates supplied via magic states dominate space-time cost, while Clifford gates incur negligible overhead. Conventional flows minimize AND count in an {XOR, AND, NOT} basis as a proxy for T, which neglects phase cancellation and can be far from T-optimal. We instead formulate an exact T synthesis problem and canonicalize Boolean functions under Clifford equivalence. By precomputing T-optimal implementations up to seven variables and developing a customized mapper, we reduce the T count by up to 14.3% on EPFL benchmarks and improve the T counts of several cryptographic modules by up to 40%.
summary: This paper presents a novel, quantum-aware approach to synthesizing quantum circuits by accurately calculating the minimum required T gate count. It overcomes the limitations of previous methods that used simple proxies for cost by formulating the problem exactly using phase polynomials. The resulting customized mapper significantly reduces the T gate count for critical quantum applications.
main_problem: The primary challenge is minimizing the T gate count during fault-tolerant quantum circuit synthesis, as T gates are the dominant resource bottleneck in quantum error correction.
main_result: The proposed method achieves significant T count reductions (up to 14.3% on EPFL benchmarks and 40% for cryptographic modules) by using an exact T cost model instead of inaccurate proxies like AND count.
method: The authors formulate an exact T synthesis problem, canonicalize Boolean functions under Clifford equivalence, and develop a customized mapper that integrates this quantum-aware cost model into the standard XAG synthesis flow.
model_or_system: The work models quantum circuits implementing Boolean functions, specifically focusing on bilinear functions, and utilizes the phase polynomial representation to capture the exact non-Clifford (T gate) cost.
key_observables: T count (the primary cost metric), AND count/Multiplicative Complexity (the inaccurate proxy), and the phase polynomial vector.
important_parameters: The maximum number of variables supported for precomputation is seven; the complexity is related to the rank of the multiplication tensor.
main_assumptions: Existing methods incorrectly assume that the AND count is a reliable proxy for the true T gate cost, which the paper refutes.
figures_summary: Figures compare T-count reductions across arithmetic benchmarks (e.g., GF(2^n) multipliers) using the new mapper versus prior state-of-the-art methods, and detail the structure of the synthesis flow and cost analysis.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from identifying the T-gate bottleneck, detailing the mathematical formulation of the exact T synthesis problem via phase polynomials, proposing the customized mapper that respects bilinear structure, and finally demonstrating the end-to-end synthesis flow and performance gains.
why_interesting: While focused on digital quantum computation, the rigorous mathematical framework for minimizing non-Clifford resources (T gates) by analyzing underlying algebraic structures (phase polynomials) is highly relevant to understanding resource constraints in any complex quantum system.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Example of the same AND count results in different T costs. Although both functions A and B contain two ANDs, the two Ts in A can be merged, while a Hadamard blocks those in B.

Figure 3: Tensor representation for bilinear Boolean functions. We present an example with a function with 𝑛= 4 inputs and 𝑚= 2 outputs. Algebraic normal form (ANF) is derived according to Equa- tion (1). Compared to the classical multiplication tensor, the phase polynomial tensor incorporates the entries that entangle inputs and outputs (highlighted in green). T can operate in this highlighted corner, where a classical bilinear operator cannot.
Figure 2: A circuit with two Ts (𝑇1, 𝑇2) and two representations of the phase shift on |𝑥1𝑥2𝑥3⟩: (a) phase polynomial and its (b) vector form.
Figure 4: Example of a multiplication tensor. The MC is given by the number of terms to decompose the tensor S. Here MC = 2.
Figure 5: Exact T synthesis example. The solver finds 𝑠with mini- mum ∥𝑠∥0 satisfying Φ · 𝑠= 𝑝𝑓(mod 2), where Φ maps parities to monomials. Selected vectors are highlighted in green.
